company_persona: a corporate travel and expense platform
title: How much should the agent do on its own?
prompt: You're a PM at Trailfin, a travel-and-expense platform. The company wants an AI agent that files expenses for employees — it reads receipts, categorizes line items, builds the report, and submits it. The team is split on one question: how much should the agent do on its own? Design the product and take a position on autonomy.

hidden_facts.error_rate: Receipt extraction (OCR + LLM) gets about 4% of line items wrong — amount, currency, or category — measured on a labeled set.
hidden_facts.volume: About 1.2 million expense reports per quarter across roughly 4,000 companies.
hidden_facts.bounce_baseline: Today, ~30% of human-filed reports get bounced back by finance for policy violations or missing data.
hidden_facts.user_research: Employees hate filing (median 25 minutes/month) but their stated fear is 'the AI submitted something wrong under my name'. Finance admins fear fraud and loss of control.
hidden_facts.stakes: A wrong submission can breach company expense policy or tax rules; finance teams audit roughly 10% of reports.
hidden_facts.customer_range: Customers span 20-person startups to 30,000-employee enterprises with custom policy rules per company.
hidden_facts.existing_asset: The platform already has a deterministic, per-customer policy rules engine (limits, categories, receipt requirements).
hidden_facts.competitor_incident: A competitor shipped full 'auto-file' and had a public embarrassment when it misfiled an executive's expenses; buyers now ask about it in sales calls.
hidden_facts.input_channels: Receipts arrive via email forwarding, phone photos, and corporate card feeds; card feeds carry structured merchant data.
hidden_facts.trust_data: In a prototype study, users who saw WHY a line item was categorized (receipt region highlighted) accepted suggestions 2.3x more often.

ideal_answer_notes: Reframe autonomy as a spectrum, not a binary: draft-only → one-tap approve → auto-file below thresholds → full auto. The design question is where each transaction lands, decided by risk, not a single global setting.
Map the stakeholders separately: employee (time back), finance admin (control, auditability), the company (compliance, fraud). Success and fears differ; the product needs a face for each.

Use the existing deterministic policy engine as the guardrail layer: the LLM extracts and proposes, rules validate. Don't ask a probabilistic model to be the compliance layer when a rules engine already exists.

Do the error math out loud: 4% line-item errors on 1.2M reports/quarter means tens of thousands of wrong items per quarter — full auto at launch is indefensible; graduated autonomy is a quantitative conclusion, not a vibe.

Propose earned autonomy: auto-file only when extraction confidence is high AND the policy engine passes AND the amount is under a per-company threshold; everything else becomes a prepared draft. Thresholds are per-customer config because a startup and a 30k-seat enterprise price risk differently.
Design for trust mechanics: show the receipt-region highlight (the 2.3x acceptance fact), an undo window before submission, and a complete activity log for finance audits.

Define success metrics per stakeholder: median time-to-file, % reports auto-approved without bounce (against the 30% human baseline), override/correction rate, finance trust survey. Guardrails: misfile rate, fraud flags, support tickets about the agent.
Rollout: opt-in per company starting in draft mode; autonomy expands per customer as measured accuracy clears bars — turning the competitor's public failure into a sales asset.
Close the loop: every human correction becomes labeled eval data (respecting per-customer data boundaries), so the accuracy that unlocks autonomy is measured, not assumed.
extra_checklist: Treats autonomy as a graduated, risk-based spectrum with explicit thresholds rather than an on/off decision
Separates probabilistic extraction (model) from deterministic policy validation (existing rules engine)
Quantifies the error budget from the 4% error rate and 1.2M report volume before choosing the default autonomy level

anchors.strong: Immediately splits stakeholders, converts the 4% error rate into an absolute error volume to reject full-auto, designs a confidence-plus-rules-plus-threshold gate with per-customer configuration, uses the receipt-highlight evidence for trust UX, and defines per-stakeholder metrics with an earned-autonomy rollout.
anchors.at_bar: Lands on 'start with human review, automate later' with some reasoning, mentions the rules engine or thresholds when nudged, and names time-saved and error-rate metrics, but doesn't quantify the error budget or differentiate customers by risk tolerance.
anchors.weak: Picks 'full auto because that's the vision' or 'always human review' without using the volume, error-rate, or research facts, ignores the finance stakeholder, and describes UI features with no guardrail metrics or rollout plan.
